exemplar.title:
An investigation into the effect of contextual information
on memory recall and comprehension

detailedExemplarData.title:
An investigation into the effect of contextual information
on memory recall and comprehension

subject.title: Psychology
subject.description:
IB Psychology is a Group 3 subject in the International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP). The course examines human behavior and mental processes through scientific inquiry, exploring biological, cognitive, and sociocultural approaches to understanding behavior. Students develop critical thinking skills while investigating contemporary psychological research, research methods, and ethical considerations in psychological practice.

The course is available at Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours) and Higher Level (HL, 240 hours), with first assessment in 2027.

Assessment consists of Paper 1 (integration of concepts, content, and contexts through short-answer and extended-response questions: SL 2 hours, HL 2 hours, SL 50%, HL 40%), Paper 2 (application of psychological knowledge to research contexts: SL 1 hour, HL 2 hours, SL 25%, HL 20%), Paper 3 (HL only: research methods and data analysis, 1 hour, 20%), and Internal Assessment (research proposal development: SL 25%, HL 20%).

strengths: Clear, specific aim linked to Bransford & Johnson paradigm and justified in an educational context
Accurate description of schema theory with explicit connection to the investigation
Precise operationalization of IV and DV, with directional and null hypotheses formally stated
weaknesses: Slight over-detail on schema subtypes not used later, which dilutes focus
Educational relevance justification is concise but could be expanded
Minor interruption in aim statementâs operational detail
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Two strands meet the top band descriptors fully (theory/model linkage and hypothesis operationalization) and the aim/relevance is very strong, warranting a band-5 rating. Minor lapses in focus and brevity prevent awarding the maximum of 6.

strengths: Research design is clearly identified and well justified (independent measures to avoid order effects)
Participant characteristics (N, age range, gender, language background) are described with some rationale for sampling
Key controls and materials are listed, and core choices (audio recording, double-marking) are explained
weaknesses: Rationale for opportunity sampling does not address limitations on statistical power or generalizability
Several controls (e.g., separate seating, timing protocol) lack explicit linkage to specific confounds
Justification for rejecting digital forms is mentioned but not fully explained
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All three strands reach the âexplainedâ level (band 3â4) but fall short of consistently robust justification across sampling and materials. A score of 3 reflects solid middle-band performance.

strengths: Appropriate use of descriptive (mean, SD) and inferential (ShapiroâWilk, oneâtailed t-test) statistics
Graphical presentation (bar chart with means and error bars) addresses the hypothesis
Findings are linked to the hypothesis with clear reporting of t, df, and p
weaknesses: Choice of one-tailed t-test is not justified with respect to Type I error risk
No formal effect size or confidence interval is reported to support practical significance
Axes on the graph lack explicit units and full labeling
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Descriptive and inferential methods are correctly applied and interpreted, but methodological caveats (one-tailed justification), missing effectâsize metrics, and minor graphing oversights place the work in the solid band 3â4 rather than at the top of band 5â6.

strengths: Findings are discussed with reference to schema theory and linked back to Bransford & Johnsonâs paradigm
Strengths and limitations are stated, explained, and relevant to the studyâs design
Proposed modifications are explicitly linked to limitations and justified
weaknesses: Discussion is concise and does not explore alternative explanations (e.g., motivational or attentional factors)
Breadth of evaluation could be expanded (e.g., cultural schema differences, inter-rater reliability)
The link to broader educational applications is mentioned but not developed
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The evaluation meets many top-band descriptors, but its narrow focus and lack of alternative perspectives moderate the performance to the solid band 3â4, warranting a score of 4.

strandSelections.reasoning: The bar chart is correctly selected for comparing two group means, clearly labels conditions, includes numerical mean values and SD error bars, and directly addresses the hypothesis.  Axis titles and legend are present, but axis units are implicit rather than explicitly labelled (âMean IUs recalledâ suffices).  Minor formatting imperfections keep the score at 5 rather than 6.

The student reports the mean difference, the t-value, p-value, and states that the null hypothesis is rejected, explicitly linking the statistical outcome to the hypothesis.  They also interpret variance through SD comparison.  Interpretation is accurate and grounded in the data.  A deeper discussion of effect size would be needed for a 6, so 5 is appropriate.

Both IV and DV are clearly identified and fully operationalised: IV = presence/absence of a specific image shown 30 s before the passage; DV = number of correctly recalled idea units out of 14 within a 4-minute written recall period.  A directional research hypothesis (Hâ) and a null hypothesis (Hâ) are formally stated.  Operationalisation is precise and quantifiable, matching the highest band; no omissions are evident, so the maximum score of 6 is justified.

Opportunity sampling is identified and participant characteristics (N = 18, age, gender mix, language background) are provided.  The student explains convenience and generalisability benefits and mentions random assignment to conditions.  However, the rationale does not fully address limitations introduced by opportunity sampling or sample size, so the explanation is adequate but less thorough than for the top of the band.  A score of 3 appropriately reflects this middle-band quality.

Schema theory is accurately described (origin, function, and sub-types) and its link to memory encoding and retrieval is set out.  The student explicitly connects Bransford & Johnsonâs findings to schema theory and then to the present investigation, explaining why contextual images should improve recall.  Description and linkage are therefore both present.  Minor tangential detail (e.g., listing four schema types that are not used later) slightly weakens focus, so a 5âstrong but not flawlessâis awarded.

Findings are discussed in relation to schema theory, explaining how visual context activates schemas to improve encoding, and differences from the original study are considered.  The discussion is coherent but relatively brief and does not engage with alternative explanations or wider literature, suiting the middle band with the higher score of 4.

The student states a clear, specific aim: to investigate the effect of prior visual context on recall of oral information, explicitly rooted in the Bransford & Johnson paradigm.  The paragraph that follows explains why this issue matters for educational practice, noting how prior knowledge can help students comprehend unfamiliar academic material.  The relevance is therefore not merely identified but explicitly justified in a real-world context, fulfilling the descriptor for the top band.  Minor brevity in the educational justification prevents awarding the very top mark of 6, so a 5 is most appropriate.

Multiple strengths (standardisation, independent-measures design, multicultural sample) and limitations (subjectivity of IU scoring, individual attentional differences, language proficiency issues) are each explained and explicitly linked to how they may have affected results.  Coverage is thorough, but a few points (e.g., small sample size) are not fully developed, so a 5 is warranted rather than 6.

Key controlled variables (separate testing, seating, pre-recorded audio, double-marking) are listed, and materials are justified (image, audio, blank paper, IU mark scheme).  Explanations for several choices are given, but some controls (e.g., timing of recall) are listed without elaboration.  The response therefore meets the âexplainedâ criterion but not with exhaustive depthâmeriting a 3 within the 3-4 band.

For each stated limitation the student proposes a concrete, justified modification (objective questionnaire, attention task, quota sampling) and explains how it would remedy the issue.  The links are explicit and logical, meeting top-band descriptors.  Slightly limited breadth (only three modifications) prevents the maximum mark of 6; thus a 5 is most fitting.

The student identifies an independent-measures design and explains WHY it was chosen (avoiding practice and fatigue effects) and contrasts it with repeated measures.  This rationale constitutes an explanation rather than a mere description, satisfying the 3-4 band.  The explanation is concise but complete, so the upper value of 4 is appropriate.

Descriptive statistics (mean, SD) and appropriate inferential statistics (Shapiro-Wilk normality tests, unpaired one-tailed t-test) are applied correctly to the independent-measures design.  Calculations are consistent with the raw data and degrees of freedom are correctly reported.  The only minor concern is the choice of a one-tailed test without a justification of increased risk of Type I error, so full accuracy with a small methodological caveat earns 5 rather than 6.

annotations.comment: The background on cognitive psychology provides a solid conceptual context, clearly situating memory recall within the broader field. This effectively orients the reader to the investigationâs theoretical grounding.
The description of schema theory is accurate but includes four schema types, some of which are not used later. Focus on the specific schemas relevant to your study to sharpen theoretical linkage.

The summary of Bransford & Johnsonâs original study is informative but could better link directly back to your altered design. Explicitly state how the simplification to two conditions maps onto the original variations.
The aim statement is cut off mid-sentence and lacks the operational detail on how the independent variable is manipulated. Complete this with precise operationalisation to avoid ambiguity.
The relevance to educational practice is concisely justified, linking prior context to studentsâ comprehension challenges. This enhances the aimâs real-world significance.
The independent and dependent variables are clearly operationalised with timing and scoring thresholds specified. This meets the highest standard for variable definition.
Operationalisation of recall as number of IUs out of 14 within four minutes is precise. This quantification strengthens the hypothesis testing framework.
The directional research and null hypotheses are formally stated. Consider adding a brief rationale for the directional choice to justify the one-tailed test.
The independent-measures design is well explained with clear rationale showing avoidance of order and fatigue effects. This meets the âexplainedâ criterion.
Opportunity sampling and participant demographics are described, but the rationale does not address the limitation of small sample size on statistical power. Elaborate this point.
Controls are comprehensively listed, but several lack explicit justification (e.g., separate seating, double-marking). Explain how each control mitigates specific confounds.
The standardized instruction recording and blank paper choice are justified, but consider explaining why digital forms were rejected beyond potential research access.
Procedure description is clear and replicable, presenting step-by-step instructions. This thoroughness enhances the reliability and transparency of the study.
The ShapiroâWilk test and outlier checks are appropriate, but the choice of a one-tailed t-test lacks justification of increased Type I risk. Address this methodological caveat.
Table 1 clearly presents means and SDs, but reporting confidence intervals or effect size (e.g., Cohenâs d) would strengthen interpretation of practical significance.
Interpretation of the mean difference and SD is accurate, but including a formal effect size metric would deepen understanding of the magnitude of the context effect.
The redundant mention of variance in successive sentences could be streamlined; focus on one concise summary of variability to improve flow.
Figure 1 visually addresses the hypothesis with correct bar chart format, but axes lack explicit units. Label axes with âMean IUs recalledâ and time parameters.
The statistical conclusion clearly links t(16)=2.1822, p<0.05 with rejection of Hâ. This ties inferential analysis directly to the hypothesis.
Discussion links findings back to schema theory, but does not consider alternative explanations (e.g., motivational or attentional factors). Broaden discussion.
Comparing your findings to the original studyâs greater IU difference provides useful context. This strengthens discussion by situating results within prior research.
The proposed questionnaire modification addresses subjectivity in IU scoring well. Additionally, consider calculating inter-rater reliability metrics to ensure grading consistency.
The independent-measures design evaluation outlines strengths and limitations effectively. To deepen evaluation, discuss potential participant differences in cognitive ability more explicitly.
You identify English proficiency variability as a limitation and propose quota sampling. How will you operationally assess participantsâ proficiency before recruitment?
The conclusion restates schema influence on encoding and retrieval succinctly. To strengthen, link back to broader learning implications and potential educational applications.
The brief summary of sampling generalizability to English-speaking students is clear, but consider addressing cultural factors that might influence schema familiarity.
